chapters.title: Social Proof and Authority Can Hijack Judgment

summaryBullets: Seeing how groups and status cues distort independent thinking creates clearer judgment.
People copy faster than they notice when belonging pressure is active.
This matters because social pressure can turn thin evidence into false certainty.
A common mistake is mistaking popularity or authority for proof.
The pattern becomes easier to see when the opinion feels safer because respected people or large groups already endorse it.
A practical starting move is to separate the claim from the crowd, ask what the evidence would look like alone, and notice the status pressure in the room.

Beliefs often bend around rewards, career pressure, and personal payoff.

The pattern becomes easier to see when the same person changes the argument as soon as the incentives change.
A practical starting move is to ask what the speaker gains, trace how the reward shapes the position, and watch what changes when the incentive changes.
